title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text:
Welcome to the Ecosystem Condition Assessments website. This resource provides information and case studies about ecological condition in South Africa.


Overview
Understand the scope, definitions, and key concepts of ecosystem condition.


Workflow
Explore the general approach and analytical steps used in the assessments.


Biomes
Dive into specific biome assessments, including Thicket, Grassland, and others.








Note



This is a living resource and will be updated as information becomes available. While the content for this website is collated and edited by Stephni van der Merwe, many researchers contributed to content creation and are cited in each page. The views expressed are not necessarily those of the affiliated institutions.

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text: Accurately assessing ecological condition in the Albany Thicket biome presents a methodological challenge due not only to its structural heterogeneity, but also to the strong environmental gradients that shape Thicket physiognomy across its range. Strong east-west environmental gradients in rainfall, productivity and species composition mean that intact Thicket in the drier west is naturally much shorter, more open, and less evergreen than intact Thicket in the east (Vlok & Euston-Brown, 2002). As a result, vegetation that would be classified as severely degraded in the east may naturally resemble “intact” Thicket in the west. Solid, closed-canopy Thicket follows a well-documented, herbivore-driven degradation trajectory characterised by canopy collapse and transitions to pseudo-savanna states (Lloyd et al., 2002; Mills & Fey, 2004; Lechmere-Oertel et al., 2005; Lechmere-Oertel, 2023), whereas Thicket mosaics form natural bush clumps interspersed with low stature matrix vegetation, in ecotones with other biomes and thus patchy vegetation unrelated to degradation (Thompson et al., 2009; Carvalho & Campbell, 2021). There is still ongoing debate about whether these mosaics might have resulted from past overutilisation or natural vegetation gradients. Ideally, these structural differences would be treated using finer-scale community or sub-type maps. However, this level of spatial granularity is not feasible at a national scale within the SBAPP framework. Given these constraints, we extrapolated the solid-thicket condition models to the mosaic Thicket units where their ecological dynamics are sufficiently similar. Recognising that degradation signals are inherently more ambiguous in mosaic ecosystems, we explicitly quantify and map model uncertainty. These uncertainty layers allow users to identify areas where predictions in mosaics should be interpreted with caution, ensuring transparency about model limitations while still providing a coherent, biome-wide condition assessment.

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text:
Training data
The SBAPP project views ecological condition as a continuum from transformed (habitat loss) to intact. Training data consisted of collated data set of condition-labelled points assigned through expert interpretation and known field-validated examples of Thicket in different states of ecological condition across the east-west environmental gradients. Training points were targeted in piospheres around artificial watering points with clear structural changes with browsing pressure, and along fence-line contrasts because these locations provide strong, unambiguous signals of herbivore-driven degradation. The close spatial pairing of fence-line contrasts reduces confounding environmental variability and increases the confidence that observed vegetation differences are primarily the result of land use history rather than underlying abiotic gradients (see e.g. Lechmere-Oertel et al., 2008). Transformed points were manually digitised in Google Earth Engine using the basemap. Points were classified into four condition classes: intact, moderate, severe, and transformed.
Ecological interpretation of the classes followed the state-and-transition framework used by Thompson et al. (2009) and Lechmere-Oertel (2023) as follows:
1. Intact
Ecologically intact sites represent closed-canopy, structurally complete succulent/woody Thicket. These areas maintain the full complement of dominant woody species (e.g., Portulacaria afra, Euclea undulata, Pappea capensis), a deep litter layer, high aboveground biomass, and well-buffered microclimates. Soil surfaces are mostly shaded, erosion is minimal, and nutrient cycling remains functional. These sites correspond to the “untransformed” or “near-reference” state, showing no evidence of sustained herbivory-driven collapse.
2. Moderate degradation
Moderately degraded Thicket exhibits partial canopy loss and early signs of structural simplification. Palatable shrubs decline but some Thicket clumps remain intact. The soil surface begins to experience intermittent exposure, ephemeral grasses, encroacher or invasiv shrubs increase in frequency, and the litter layer becomes discontinuous. This state aligns with the intermediate transition phase in Thompson et al. (2009)’s condition gradient, where degradation is evident but reversible if grazing pressure is reduced.
3. Severe degradation
Severely degraded Thicket shows advanced canopy collapse, dominated by pseudo-savanna elements such as annual grasses, Pentzia incana, Galenia spp., and scattered remnant shrubs persisting only above the browse line. The original Thicket structure is largely lost, soil surfaces are exposed and crusted, and erosion scars are common. Nutrient cycling and microclimatic buffering have broken down. This corresponds to the high-degradation state of Thompson et al. (2009), where functional thresholds have been crossed and passive recovery is unlikely.
4. Transformed
Transformed sites represent areas where Thicket has been completely removed by ploughing, orchards, croplands, urban development, mining, or other land-cover conversion. Ecologically, these areas no longer retain Thicket structure, species composition, or processes. In short, this class is where Thicket has lost habitat by direct human transformation.

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text:
Predictor variables
Predictor variables were derived from the Google AlphaEarth Foundation satellite embeddings 10 m resolution annual dataset (available 2017-2024). Embeddings provide a way to compress large volumes of remote-sensing information into a compact set of features that may capture meaningful ecological and spatial patterns. The AlphaEarth Foundation Model integrates multi-temporal imagery from sensors such as Sentinel-2, Sentinel-1 and Landsat, Land Cover data, elevation and learns a shared representation of the information contained across these sources (Brown et al., 2025). This allows each pixel’s spectral, spatial and temporal characteristics to be summarised in a small embedding vector of 64 values, that captures the key mutual information between the different sensors and the underlying landscape. Embedding values were sampled at each training point, which formed the input into a Random Forest model.

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text:
Random Forest modelling
A supervised Random Forest (RF) regression model was trained separately for Arid, Valley, and Mesic Thicket using AlphaEarth embeddings as predictors and a continuous condition target derived from ordinal class labels in the training data. Ordinal class scores (0-1) were obtained by first applying Partial Least Squares (PLS) regression to the 64 embedding bands with ordered condition classes (transformed < severe < moderate < intact) as the response, thereby extracting supervised components that capture the condition gradient (see e.g. Bell et al., 2021). The number of PLS components was chosen at the RMSEP “elbow” from cross-validation and used as predictors in an ordinal logistic (cumulative link) model (Mevik & Wehrens, 2024). The resulting latent condition axis was summarised per class and rescaled to 0-1 to produce data-driven numeric values for transformed, severe, moderate, and intact points (Bell et al., 2021). These values obtained using the pls package (Mevik & Wehrens, 2024) in R Statistical Software, were then used as the response for RF regression in GEE, enabling prediction of a continuous 0-1 ecological condition surface across each solid Thicket EFG. The numeric scaling facilitates downstream analyses, comparison across EFGs, and integration with condition frameworks such as the IUCN Red List of Ecosystems (Criteria C & D) and SBAPP’s multi-pressure condition model, which both require a continuous measure of relative degradation intensity. The models were fitted using 300 trees with class-balanced sampling, applied to the embeddings for each the three solid EFGs. Outputs were clipped to the EFG boundaries to avoid extrapolation, and water bodies were masked using classes 14-21 in the SANLC 2022 dataset. The final condition gradient was exported at 10 m resolution for expert review.

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text:
Model validation
Model performance for the supervised Random Forest (RF) approach was evaluated using a combination of internal cross-validation and independent expert-mapped degradation maps. First, the condition-labelled points were randomly partitioned into an 80/20 train-test split, stratified by condition class to retain balanced representation of intact, moderate, severe and transformed sites. The model was trained on the 80% subset and predictions were evaluated against the held-out 20% using overall accuracy, class-specific precision and recall, and confusion matrices to quantify how well the RF distinguished different degradation states. To provide an external, ecologically independent assessment of model realism, we further validated predictions against farm-level degradation maps supplied by Thicket experts, mapped between 2020-2022, which contain spatially explicit delineations of moderate and severely degraded and intact areas. Points were randomly placed in areas that overlap with the expert-mapped polygons, where the RF predictions values were extracted, and agreement was assessed using mean predicted condition per polygon, class-wise accuracy, and visual consistency with known degradation patterns. This two-step validation of formal train-test evaluation complemented by expert-derived farm-scale benchmarks, ensured that the RF models not only performed well statistically, but also captured degradation patterns recognised by practitioners familiar with local Thicket dynamics.

Key pressures
Fire and grazing are the two most important factors that drive grassland ecology in South Africa. A disruption or change in fire or grazing regime may significantly alter species composition and vegetation structure. Alien invasive densification and soil erosion are

title: Ecosystem Condition Assessments
text:
Key indicators
Herbaceous biomass is also the key predictor of herbivore biomass and the key indicators of overgrazing in rangelands in Grassland are woody increase, such as Seriphium plumosum density increase, or bare soil increase (which may lead to soil erosion). Therefore, knowing the proportions of land covered by herbaceous vegetation, trees/shrubs and bare soil, already brings us a step closer to more accurately assessing ecosystem condition. Calibrating the spectral sigantures to each bioregion’s unique species composition (and thus potentially unique colours) then improves the accuracy of assessing ecosystem condition.
Fractional vegetation cover (FVG) can be used to assess ecosystem condition in grassy biomes. Using the fraction of tree, shrub, photosynthetically active herbaceous vegetation and bare soil, we can estimate grassland or savanna integrity, if calibrated to each ecoregion. The calibration is necessary, because the spectral signature of the vegetation may be different in the different eocregions, due to species composition. For instance, the spectral signature of a mesic grassland dominated by x, may be significantly different to a dry highveld grassland dominated by y.

title: Key concepts
text:
This section provides an overview of the scope, key concepts and definitions used in this website. Due to the broad nature of ecological condition research, we clearly define what ecosystem condition, degradation and related terms, but are aware that often these terms are used interchangeably in the literature.

title: Key concepts
text:
Key Concepts

Condition: The state of the ecosystem relative to a reference intact state.
Assessment: The process of evaluating condition.

title: Thicket Biome
text:
The Thicket Biome is a structurally complex and compositionally diverse vegetation that occupies a transitional zone between the country’s arid interior and its more mesic coastal regions (Vlok et al. 2003; Hoare et al. 2006). Thicket vegetation varies along key environmental gradients of moisture availability, soil type, topography, and rainfall seasonality, giving rise to a variety of growth forms occurring in a mosaic of distinct vegetation clumps (Vlok et al., 2003). At the EFG level, along the coastal fringe, Dune Thicket occurs on aeolian sands with high wind exposure and saline influences. This vegetation is characterised by a dense woody canopy, low succulent presence, and species adapted to sand stabilisation and frequent disturbance (SANBI, 2021; Strydom et al., 2022). Inland from the coast, Valley Thicket is found along major river systems and in topographic hollows with elevated soil moisture and fertile, shale-derived soils (Vlok et al. 2003). These systems support a rich assemblage of woody and succulent species and are notable for their resistance to seasonal climatic fluctuations (Rutherford et al., 2014; SANBI 2021). In regions with moderate, year-round rainfall, Mesic Thicket occupies fire-protected topographic refugia such as deep valleys and southern slopes. It forms a dense, stratified canopy of evergreen and weakly deciduous shrubs and trees similar to forest vegetation, and plays a critical role in soil retention and carbon sequestration (Lechmere-Oertel et al., 2005; SANBI, 2021). By contrast, Arid Thicket dominates the interior valleys and escarpment foothills, where rainfall is low and erratic, and vegetation is shaped by chronic water deficit, nutrient-poor soils, and intense grazing pressure (van Luijk et al., 2013; Rutherford et al., 2014). Arid Thicket is distinguished from other thicket forms by a poorly developed woody component and a prominent succulent layer, with communities being either dominated by Portulacaria afra (eg. in Spekboomveld) or by other succulents (eg. in Noorsveld). All the EFGs also occur in mosaics with other biomes.
Intact solid Thicket vegetation, i.e. untransformed and not in mosaics or bush clumps, is characterised by its resilience to certain disturbances, such as fire and herbivory, due to its dense near-impenetrable canopy and thorny structure (Vlok et al., 2003). However, this resilience comes at a cost: once disturbed, Thicket vegetation exhibits a low regenerative capacity, often failing to return to its original stable state or requiring decades for recovery of the slow-growing species (Lechmere-Oertel et al., 2005; Carvalho et al., 2022). This has significant implications for conservation, as the remaining intact Thicket needs to be prioritised for protection, and degraded sites actively restored where possible (Lechmere-Oertel, 2023). Indeed, restoration efforts in the Thicket has shown remarkable success when given space and time for recovery in protected sites (Mills & Cowling, 2006; del Rio-Mena et al., 2021; Galuszynski, 2023).



The degradation of Thicket has been ongoing for over a century. Historical accounts of vast areas of dense, thorny and impenetrable vegetation (Hoare et al., 2006; Reardon, 2021), are rarely seen today. Other than outright habitat loss due to urban development and croplands, the replacement of indigenous megaherbivores (like elephants) with high densities of domestic stock has caused severe degradation of the natural landscapes (Lechmere-Oertel et al., 2005; Rutherford et al., 2014)​. Historical overstocking of livestock has thus likely been the primary driver of vegetation change in the arid parts of this biome over the past 200 years, outweighing natural climatic fluctuations (Thompson et al., 2009)​. Inside protected areas such as Addo Elephant National Park, browsing by elephants also impacts thicket structure (e.g. converting tall thicket to a shorter, more open state), but it is a more localised effect compared to the widespread clearing for citrus planting or unsustainable herbivory in rangelands. Outside these refuges, many thicket landscapes are in various stages of degradation, with reduced shrub or succulent cover, altered species composition, and diminished ecological function (lower carbon stocks and soil stability), often being indicators of degradation (van Luijk et al., 2013; Rutherford et al., 2014)​.
The Thicket biome faces increasing pressure from anthropogenic activities, particularly overgrazing/browsing by livestock such as goats or extralimital game species, which has led to severe degradation, such as loss of key plant species and canopy cover, increasing grass cover and soil erosion in many areas (Lechmere-Oertel, 2023). A recent study found that structural changes to Arid Thicket are linked to functional changes to new vegetation states with differing herbivory regimes (Schmidt & Kerley, 2024). Soil erosion, largely driven by unsustainable herbivory, contributes to the loss of topsoil and further diminishes the ecological integrity of these areas by reducing regenerative capacity (Lechmere-Oertel et al., 2005), ultimately resulting in carbon loss (Mills & Cowling, 2006). Invasive alien species, such as Opuntia species and Australian wattles, further exacerbate this degradation by outcompeting native vegetation and changing ecosystem processes through altered nutrient cycling, hydrology and fire regimes (Veldtman et al., 2010; Kraaij et al., 2022). Additionally, climate change poses a long-term threat to the Thicket biome, with shifts in temperature and precipitation patterns potentially altering the distribution and composition of vegetation, as climatic niches shift. Droughts in particular are predicted to increase in South Africa, and pose a major threat to the ecological condition of Thicket. These change drivers interact in complex ways, often exacerbating the impacts on Thicket ecosystems and making conservation and restoration efforts more challenging (Lloyd et al. 2002). However, understanding and mitigating these drivers are crucial for preserving the ecological integrity of Thicket vegetation.



Alastair Potts, Anthony Palmer and Michael Powell assisted the SBAPP project team for the Albany Thicket biome.

title: Thicket Biome
text:
The Thicket Biome is a structurally complex and compositionally diverse vegetation that occupies a transitional zone between the country’s arid interior and its more mesic coastal regions (Vlok et al. 2003; Hoare et al. 2006). Thicket vegetation varies along key environmental gradients of moisture availability, soil type, topography, and rainfall seasonality, giving rise to a variety of growth forms occurring in a mosaic of distinct vegetation clumps (Vlok et al., 2003). These change drivers interact in complex ways, often exacerbating the impacts on Thicket ecosystems and making conservation and restoration efforts more challenging (Lloyd et al. 2002). However, understanding and mitigating these drivers are crucial for preserving the ecological integrity of Thicket vegetation.



Alastair Potts, Anthony Palmer and Michael Powell assisted the SBAPP project team for the Albany Thicket biome.

title: Workflow
text:
This section outlines the workflow used for the assessments.






Process




Steps workflow diagram





Overall the conceptual models in each bioregion helps to interpret and identify the ecological drivers of different stable states, to differentiate intact vegetation states from degraded states.
Palatability has been a key concept for decades used to inform degradation of natural vegetation in most biomes, especially in the Grassland and Nama Karoo. Continued selective grazing often leads to a transition from palatable perennial species to unpalatable and short-lived species. In some cases, this change may be detected with satellite imagery as colour or phenological shifts, if the altered species composition is markedly different.
Depending on the biome, different analytical steps are taken.




The guidelines were used to prioritise the key pressures and degradation processes most relevant to assessing the ecological condition in each biome.
This section outlines the workflow used for the assessments.






History of mapping degradation in South Africa



The utility of using remote sensing to map degradation was recognised as soon as the images became available in the 1970s. Several studies aimed to relate vegetation indices derived from satellite images, especially NDVI, to field-observed contrasts in rangeland condition. Analyses transitioned to time series as more satellite data were released and by the 2000s vegetation productivity trends became widely used proxies for degradation. Some recent mapping attempts have made significant progress toward mapping ecological condition. Most global products’ resolution is inappropriate for relating it to ecological condition at the local scale needed for heterogenous ecosystems in South Africa. Though 1m products are now emerging, the accuracy remains low for South Africa, due to lack of ground truthing, which limits the use for land-use decision making. Therefore, we often still have to create our own condition data that is developed with the intention of conducting condition assessments from the outset.








The contentious nature of selecting a baseline condition



Ecologists rarely define baseline condition due to human influences being present for centuries, making it near impossible to observe a truly intact state of vegetation. In practice however, environmental impact assessment and restoration practitioners must have reference states to compare to. Similarly, to conduct spatial biodiversity assessments, a reference state is needed for relative coparison. Therefore, it is ideal to select the closest possible modern manifestation of an ecosystem type, based on consensus views. Conservation areas that have been protected for decades are often the only remnants left as intact examples of ecosystem types.

title: Workflow
text:
This section outlines the workflow used for the assessments.






Process




Steps workflow diagram





Overall the conceptual models in each bioregion helps to interpret and identify the ecological drivers of different stable states, to differentiate intact vegetation states from degraded states.
Palatability has been a key concept for decades used to inform degradation of natural vegetation in most biomes, especially in the Grassland and Nama Karoo. Continued selective grazing often leads to a transition from palatable perennial species to unpalatable and short-lived species. In some cases, this change may be detected with satellite imagery as colour or phenological shifts, if the altered species composition is markedly different.
Depending on the biome, different analytical steps are taken.




The guidelines were used to prioritise the key pressures and degradation processes most relevant to assessing the ecological condition in each biome.
This section outlines the workflow used for the assessments.






History of mapping degradation in South Africa



The utility of using remote sensing to map degradation was recognised as soon as the images became available in the 1970s. Several studies aimed to relate vegetation indices derived from satellite images, especially NDVI, to field-observed contrasts in rangeland condition. Analyses transitioned to time series as more satellite data were released and by the 2000s vegetation productivity trends became widely used proxies for degradation. Some recent mapping attempts have made significant progress toward mapping ecological condition. Most global products’ resolution is inappropriate for relating it to ecological condition at the local scale needed for heterogenous ecosystems in South Africa. Though 1m products are now emerging, the accuracy remains low for South Africa, due to lack of ground truthing, which limits the use for land-use decision making. Therefore, we often still have to create our own condition data that is developed with the intention of conducting condition assessments from the outset.








The contentious nature of selecting a baseline condition



Ecologists rarely define baseline condition due to human influences being present for centuries, making it near impossible to observe a truly intact state of vegetation. In practice however, environmental impact assessment and restoration practitioners must have reference states to compare to. Similarly, to conduct spatial biodiversity assessments, a reference state is needed for relative coparison. Therefore, it is ideal to select the closest possible modern manifestation of an ecosystem type, based on consensus views. Conservation areas that have been protected for decades are often the only remnants left as intact examples of ecosystem types.
